data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1153 ET - Intel's new CEO Lip-Bu Tan is well-equipped to meet the demands of the job but questions about strategic shifts remain, say analysts at Deutsche Bank in a research note. The beleaguered semiconductor company on Wednesday selected Tan, a former board member and longtime industry executive, as its new CEO. The analysts question whether Tan supports Intel in its current form or would forge ahead with splitting up the business, as has been a rumored possibility for the company. "All told, we are pleased to see this leadership transition occur in a timely fashion," say the analysts. "We look forward to monitoring any strategic shifts he may undertake going forward, but as we have cited in numerous notes, nearly every strategic choice will require patience." (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1141 ET - Intel's new CEO Lip-Bu Tan was perhaps the best of an extremely short list of candidates that could help potential customers in both major divisions give the company another look, say analysts at Melius Research in a research note. The beleaguered semiconductor company on Wednesday selected Tan, a former board member and longtime industry executive, as its new CEO. The analysts don't expect any changes to cost-cutting measures under way at Intel based on Tan's comments in a memo to employees. In it, the analysts believe Tan is talking about refocusing on GPUs and AI where he notes it's "behind the competition." Tan also said Intel needs to pick up the pace, a nod to his frustration at the slowness at the company that prompted him to leave the board. Shares rise 15%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1108 ET - Intel has a greater opportunity to restructure and turn things around the new leadership of Lip-Bu Tan, say analysts at BofA Securities in a research note. The beleaguered semiconductor company on Wednesday selected Tan, a former board member and longtime industry executive, as its new CEO. The analysts cite Tan's track record of success at Cadence Design Systems, among other factors in why they like the pick. They also think improving market conditions might help Intel divest certain assets, which would delever its balance sheet. "Intel's material turnaround remains a work in progress, but if executed well could be [an] incremental headwind for peer AMD," say the analysts. Shares surge 15% following the appointment. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1016 ET - CreditSights analysts advise investors to exercise caution while investing in corporate bonds within the satellite sector. The analysts have a weak outlook on most legacy players in the industry, even as the sector is drawing investor interest due to potentially higher defense spending in Europe. Some longstanding firms have struggled to keep up with new developments and have a hard time cutting costs, CreditSights says. "We continue to be cautious on satellite but it is all about credit selection." CreditSights has a buy recommendation on the bonds of Hughes Networks Systems; has a market perform rating on Eutelsat Communications and Inmarsat; and underperform ratings on Viasat, SES and Telesat. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0753 ET - Deliveroo's cash flow milestone was offset by its bleak outlook, AJ Bell's Russ Mould says. For 2024, the London-listed company reported full-year profit and positive free cash flow for the first time. Deliveroo flagged that the consumer environment remains uncertain, as people need to have disposable income to splurge on takeaway, he writes in a note. The company doesn't look well-placed to face an already competitive landscape that recently got even tricker as Just Eat Takeaway is set to be acquired by Prosus, Mould adds. However, the British food-delivery company's recent decision to exit the Hong Kong market was probably the right one, the analyst says. Shares are down 6.3% at 116.80 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0701 ET - Intel's appointment of Lip-Bu Tan as chief executive gives hope that the chip maker can be kept alive despite continually losing market share to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Quilter Cheviot's Ben Barringer writes in a research note. "Tan is a highly respected individual, but he has his work cut out turning around the fortunes of Intel and making it competitive once again," he says. Former CEO Pat Gelsinger pursued a costly plan to double down on building new factories to catch up with rivals. That move was blamed for ultimately wiping out $150 billion in market value since his appointment as CEO in 2021. Intel shares are up 11% premarket at $23.04. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0539 ET - Intel's appointment of Lip-Bu Tan as its new chief executive is a positive development as decisions on semiconductor manufacturing can now be made fairly soon, JPMorgan analysts write in a research note. "In our conversations with clients, the recent lack of direction at Intel has been an important reason for the market's caution on semiconductor equipment suppliers," they say. While decisions at Intel may take a few months to be announced, the analysts note Tan was on Intel's board until the summer of 2024 and is aware of the issues the chip maker faces. Intel shares are up 10% premarket at $22.74. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0527 ET - Intel shares climb premarket after the chip maker appointed Lip-Bu Tan as its new chief executive. Intel stock closed 4.6% higher at $20.68 on Wednesday. Shares are up 9.9% Thursday premarket, touching a high of $22.98. Pat Gelsinger, the company's former CEO, retired abruptly in December as the chip maker lagged behind rivals like Nvidia in the AI race. Tan, the former CEO of Cadence Design Systems said he would seek to restore Intel's position in the semiconductor industry. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0522 ET - Telefonica will likely benefit from the sale of its majority stake in Colombian business to Millicom, ING analyst Jan Frederik Slijkerman writes in a note. The transaction will allow the Spanish telecommunications company to focus its strategy on more profitable markets, providing also a reduced exposure to non-euro currencies, Slijkerman says. The agreed price for the shares looks fair, he adds. Additionally, "Telefonica is still entitled to the proceeds of a 523 million euros claim from a longstanding dispute with the Colombian government and it would be nice to have this confirmed," he adds. In November 2024, the International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes ruled in favor of Telefonica. Shares are up 1.3% at 4.28 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0355 ET - One way for China to rebuff Trump's tariff threat is to develop its own critical technologies, Natixis analysts write in a note. China has used the Two Sessions meeting to respond to Trump's policies, they say. As China has introduced additional measures to support the development of critical technologies, especially artificial intelligence, that could help the country further reduce its technological dependence on U.S., they add. DeepSeek's success in late January has instigated a recovery in stock markets in China and Hong Kong, they note. Supportive measures by Beijing could furthersustain the positive sentiment on China's tech sector, Natixis says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

2229 ET - Naver's commission adjustments, effective June 2, are set to boost revenue and earnings, Nomura analysts Angela Hong and Won Kang write in a note. The South Korean internet platform giant's new take-rate structure of reduced commissions can lead sellers to spend more on advertising, they say. The company has room to improve its e-commerce monetization given its low rates of commissions to sales, they note. They raise their earnings-per-share estimates for Naver by 4%-15% for 2025-2026. Nomura upgrades its rating on the stock to buy from neutral and raises its target price by 18% to KRW260,000. Shares are 1.4% higher at KRW217,000. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2054 ET - Macquarie analysts turn bearish on Australia's Audinate, citing potential headwinds including trade tariffs. Cutting their recommendation on the stock to underperform from neutral, the analysts tell clients in a note that they see no evidence for optimism on the audio-visual equipment maker. They point to softer economic growth, tariffs, weak market data, and continued discounting of adaptors to support their view. Management is responding soundly to softer demand but these market conditions are set to persist, they add. Macquarie cuts its target price 28% to A$6.30. Shares are down 0.7% at A$6.76. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1019 ET - Trouble in the auto industry poses a growing risk to Deutsche Bank's lending portfolio to the sector, but the impact so far is limited, the German bank says in its annual report. The lender says it identified the auto industry, as well as commercial real estate and climate risk, as focus areas in its 2024 credit-risk management assessment. The European auto industry faces challenges including potential U.S. tariffs, the transition to electric vehicles and competition from China, Deutsche Bank says. The industry's outlook remains subdued, it adds. "The difficult industry environment has limited impact on Deutsche Bank's automotive and supplier portfolio thus far with only moderate downward rating migrations observed across [a] small portfolio comprising 1.5% of the group's loan book," the bank says. Shares fall 0.3%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0604 ET - CAB Payments Holdings shares will likely remain weak until the fintech group delivers consistent growth, Peel Hunt analysts Gautam Pillai and Barun Singh write in a note. The London-listed company's 2024 results were overall weak, the analysts say. The group reported a double-digit percentage fall in key metrics, reflecting a transition year. However, market opportunities and the strong value proposition in emerging markets cross-border payments remain unchanged, the analysts add. Shares are down 5.6% at 50.60 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0432 ET - Hannover Re's finished the year strongly with its dividend increase, J.P. Morgan says in a research note. The German reinsurer declared a 9-euro dividend, up from 7.2 euros. "In our experience, Hannover Re has normally produced strong earnings numbers but has sometimes been weaker than expectations when it comes to capital returns so today's increase while in line with consensus is a pleasing development in our view," analysts write. The group has balance sheet buffers and is able to deliver smooth earnings in difficult years, they say. Shares open 2.1% higher at 277.8 euros, its highest level ever. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0400 ET - Assicurazioni Generali's dividend growth is solid, J.P. Morgan says in a research note after the Italian insurer posted 2024 results in line with expectations. The dividend payout, which grew 12% compared with the previous year, was stronger than expected, analysts write. Management reiterated its ambition to grow its dividend by more than 10% annually over the next three years and while consensus seems to already have factored in this for 2025, there is upside to consensus for 2026 and 2027, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0356 ET - Assicurazioni Generali posted a solid set of numbers, Morgan Stanley says in a research note after the Italian insurer's 2024 results. Its headline group operating profit was ahead of consensus expectations. "When looking through the detail, this appears to be a clean, high quality beat, particularly in [property and casualty]," analysts write. They note that a potential question mark will be the miss in life contractual service margin due to weaker economic and operating variances and while they expect this to be in focus in the call with management, they don't see it as enough to detract from the positives elsewhere in the print. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0352 ET - Bridgepoint's better-than-expected full-year results should boost its share price following a period of weakness, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. The British private-equity firm's Ebitda margin guidance of 52%-55% in 2025/2026 compares with consensus estimates of 50% in 2025 and 53% in 2026, they say. Bridgepoint's adjusted Ebitda topped company-compiled consensus, whereas total expenses were in line with estimates, JPMorgan says. Its adjusted profit before tax missed expectations by 7% as consensus didn't properly account for the cost of additional debt used to finance the acquisition of infrastructure investor Energy Capital Partners, as well as foreign currency headwinds, they say.(helena.smolak@wsj.com)

2120 ET - There are multiple reasons why Westpac is likely to underperform the broader Australian share market in 2025, according to the bank's bears at Morgan Stanley. They highlight eight reasons for their continued underweight rating, but point to three as the most important: margin trends, capital management and the lender's ongoing tech overhaul. The analysts point out in a note to clients that margins and capital both fell short of expectations in Westpac's first fiscal quarter, while investors are waiting on more detail about the tech overhaul benefits. Raising their expense estimates, the MS analysts cut their target price by 6.5% to A$27.30. Shares are down 1.3% at A$29.68. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1958 ET - New Zealand needs to remain open to diversifying its dairy exports, says Forsyth Barr, citing the slowdown in purchases by its top customer. China accounts for 35% of New Zealand's dairy exports, but local production has been rising at a time when demand is weak. Analyst Matt Montgomerie highlights that China's milk production has increased at a compound annual rate of 5% over the past five years. Its production relative to New Zealand is now at its highest level in two decades. "Rising China milk production and New Zealand's reliance on China (also 40% of Fonterra's EBIT excluding Consumer) remain key risks for the industry," Forsyth Barr says. China remains the weak spot in an otherwise bullish moment for New Zealand dairy producers, with milk prices at record highs and costs stabilizing.(david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1421 ET - Front-month gold settles up 0.9% to $2,939.10 a troy ounce, making it the sixth session out of the past eight that front-month gold closed with a positive finish. The escalating tariff battle seen today — with the EU instituting retaliatory tariffs and Canada adding new levies — is helping propel gold, says Peter Cardillo of Spartan Capital Securities. "We believe gold is moving higher due to the prospects of the trade war intensifying in the coming weeks," he says. "The gold market is rising in response to potential inflation stemming from the trade war and uncertainties surrounding global economic activity."SPDR Gold rises 0.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0945 ET - Oil futures are giving back some of the previous session's gains with market concerns over the demand outlook and the potential economic impact of trade tariffs weighing on sentiment. The IEA lowered its demand growth estimate for this year to 1.03 million barrels a day from 1.1 million b/d, citing trade tensions. "The tariff trade war saga continues to weigh on prices and a peace deal with Russia is very probable, and with peace will likely come added Russian crude barrels to the world market," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "Add in the latest selloff in the stock market and near-term demand remains vulnerable." WTI is down 1% at $67.03 a barrel and Brent is off 0.9% at $70.34 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

2004 ET - New Zealand investors have dropped the chance of Contact Energy securing regulatory clearance for its takeover of Manawa Energy to less than 40%, Forsyth Barr says. It believes Contact has offered a full price for Manawa, and it will take several years to deliver savings needed to justify the transaction cost. Analyst Andrew Harvey-Green doesn't expect Contact's share price to react much whatever New Zealand's Commerce Commission decides about the deal. "The situation is different for Manawa," Forsyth Barr says. "The passage of time means it is increasingly difficult to estimate the size of the market reaction to no deal." It thinks Manawa's share price could fall to around NZ$4.00 if the takeover is blocked, from NZ$4.88 currently. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1528 ET - Oil futures gain as the EIA's report of a 1.4 million barrel increase in U.S. crude oil stocks is offset by a bigger-than-expected 5.7 million drawdown in gasoline inventories. U.S. inflation for February came in lower than expected, and OPEC kept its demand growth estimates for 2025 and 2026 unchanged at 1.45 million barrels a day and 1.43 million b/d, respectively. Oil's push toward the top of its recent $3 trading range despite stock market losses "is sending off some supportive vibes suggestive of further upside," Ritterbusch says in a note. While the drop in oil prices since President Trump took office "looks successful thus far," it has been for the wrong reasons "i.e., tariff-related expectations for global oil demand deterioration," the firm adds. WTI settles up 2.2% at $67.68 a barrel, and Brent rises 2% to $70.95 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0931 ET - President Trump's focus on annexing Canada to make it the 51st U.S. state has put the northern nation's wealth of natural resources in the spotlight, but just what is that worth? Based on data from Statistics Canada, the country's mineral and energy reserves and timber combined accounted for nearly C$1.3 trillion in the last quarter, or 7.5% of the country's national wealth. Canadian exports of minerals, energy and timber represent a big contributor to its economy and to the economic activity of countries who benefit from this trade, including the U.S., the data agency notes. It says 2024 data on Canadian international merchandise trade shows selected domestic exports of minerals, energy and timber products accounted for over C$300 billion, with nearly three-quarters of this destined for the U.S. on a customs basis. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0352 ET - K+S shares are likely to trade largely flat after the company reported a set of results with both positive and negative aspects and issued a cautious outlook for 2025, analysts at Morgan Stanley say in a research note. The German fertilizer company's fourth-quarter earnings were better than consensus expectations thanks to improved agriculture volumes, but free cash flow came in weaker than anticipated, Morgan Stanley says. K+S's 2025 guidance looks cautious, according to the analysts. The mid-point of its 2025 Ebitda outlook range of between 500 million and 620 million euros is slightly above the consensus forecast, but its volume guidance disappoints largely due to demand and logistics uncertainty, Morgan Stanley says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0315 ET - Merdeka Copper Gold may benefit from two significant projects--TB Copper and Pani Gold, CGS International analysts say in a research report as they resume coverage of the stock with an add rating and target price of IDR2,400.00. TB Copper, a copper mine in Indonesia, is expected to begin production in 2028 and contribute around US$360 million Ebitda, the analysts estimate. Pani Gold, a gold mine in Indonesia, is expected to start production in 2026 and contribute US$91 million Ebitda, they estimate. With the start of these two projects, three-year Ebitda CAGR for the Indonesian producer of gold, copper and other minerals could reach 54%, the analysts add. Shares are 0.4% lower at IDR1,325.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2103 ET - Rio Tinto's production and free cash flow are likely to accelerate faster than rival BHP's over the rest of this decade, Goldman Sachs analysts say in a note. Alongside valuation, that's why GS prefers Rio's stock over BHP, both of which it rates a buy, the analysts say. Despite both miners spending around US$10 billion-US$11 billion on capex annually, Rio's copper-equivalent production should rise about 20% by the end of the decade. BHP's output is expected to remain largely flat, with its major projects mostly longer dated, say the analysts.(rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2042 ET - Orica's A$400 million share buyback confirms the explosives maker's near-term focus is on integrating recent acquisitions, and not pursuing more M&A, Macquarie analysts say in a note. Of Orica's integration efforts, "Terra is performing well following [the] successful Ground Probe acquisition whilst Cyanaco remains [a] work in progress," they say. The analysts were pleased to see the much-anticipated buyback eventuate, even if it was "a case of 'buy the rumor, sell the fact'" for investors, they say. After falling by 1.3% Wednesday, Orica's shares are up 1.3% in Sydney at A$16.895. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1853 ET - Algoma Steel Group says that tariffs on steel and aluminum will have a material and adverse effect on its finances and operations. That is because tariffs have driven steel prices below production costs for much of the industry, as well as heightened general macroeconomic uncertainty. "We remain confident that the Canadian federal and provincial governments will respond swiftly and appropriately to support the industry," CEO Michael Garcia says. "Given the deeply integrated North American supply chain, we believe rational dialogue will prevail between these close allies, restoring normal steel trade." The Canadian company is currently assessing the tariffs, adding that it is too early to estimate the full extent of their effects at this time. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1803 ET - Alkane Resources's Boda-Kaiser project in eastern Australia has become more valuable as gold prices hover near a record high, bolstering its appeal to potential investors, says Bell Potter. A scoping study released mid last year pointed to a net present value of A$1.1 billion after tax. That was based on a gold price of A$3,500/oz. Now, however, the gold price has risen to A$4,640/oz. Analyst Bradley Watson says the spot gold price suggests a post-tax net present value of A$2.1 billion. "We think the value of Boda at current gold prices will not be lost on potential partners that are scouring the gold sector for still-undervalued opportunities," says Bell Potter, which rates Alkane a buy. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1739 ET - Ramelius Resources will increasingly need to consider M&A, says Ord Minnett, after an updated plan for the Mt Magnet gold mine in Western Australia disappointed the market. The new plan highlights a steeper production fall at Mt Magnet in FY 2026-FY 2031, analyst Paul Kaner says. Ord Minnett had previously thought the Eridanus cut-back/mill expansion could plug some of this shortfall. Now, however, it appears that Ramelius will need M&A to address this, especially if its Rebecca-Roe development is also pushed out. "As a result, the market will likely place an M&A discount on the stock, regardless of track record given the higher pricing environment (i.e. seller's market)," Ord Minnett says. The bank downgrades Ramelius to accumulate, from buy. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1431 ET - Labrador Iron Ore Royalty faces lower growth and a challenging tariff landscape, TD Cowen's Craig Hutchison says in a report, downgrading the stock to hold from buy. The analyst notes a 4Q earnings miss, though cash-flow per share was above TD estimates. He also mentions sputtering steel demand as a risk amid escalating tariffs and softer global growth. Shares are down 3.4% to C$29.15, and off 3.5% in the past 12 months. TD Cowen also lowers its price target to C$31 from C$32. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1412 ET - The U.S. imposed 25% tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports earlier today, but President Trump had threatened a 50% tariff on Canadian steel and aluminum Tuesday, before relenting. That additional tariff would likely change trade flows, but not in a way that would benefit U.S. manufacturing, Alcoa CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. Under proposed higher tariffs, Alcoa would most likely send its aluminum produced in Canada to Europe, she says. "That supply may very well make its way over to the U.S.," Beerman says, adding that to have ships crossing the Atlantic with the same product just to get the right tariff treatment would be very disruptive. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1343 ET - Alcoa "would not make a decision on building a new smelter based on tariffs," CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. "These are decisions that you're looking out 20 to 40 years." The company could restart some of its idle U.S. capacity, she says, though she adds that those smelters have not been run for a very long time and would be very expensive to restart. "We are looking at the numbers but we'll want to see the cost to restart, as well as how the tariffs stick before we make a decision on whether we'll bring that back online," Beerman says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1303 ET - Alcoa has two smelters in the U.S. that produce about 290,000 metric tons of aluminum annually, both of which stand to benefit from tariffs enacted on imports of steel and aluminum, CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. "However, our Canadian operations produce three times that amount," she says. A 25% tariff on the aluminum that Alcoa produces in Canada would be a net negative for the company, resulting in the loss of tens of millions of dollars, Beerman says. The loss isn't likely to hurt the company's first quarter, but could affect future earnings, she adds. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1604 GMT - Orders for new dry-bulk ships dropped 92% year-over-year in the first two months of 2025 to the lowest in at least 30 years, shipowners' association Bimco says. While contracting was low in January, no new bulkers at all were ordered in February. "Weak freight rates, high newbuilding prices, long lead times and uncertainty are likely discouraging contracting," says Filipe Gouveia, Bimco's shipping analysis manager. Strong contracting for containerships and tankers, which use the same shipyards, has kept newbuild prices high and extended lead times, Bimco adds. (mark.long@wsj.com)

1419 GMT - Trouble in the auto industry poses a growing risk to Deutsche Bank's lending portfolio to the sector, but the impact so far is limited, the German bank says in its annual report. The lender says it identified the auto industry, as well as commercial real estate and climate risk, as focus areas in its 2024 credit-risk management assessment. The European auto industry faces challenges including potential U.S. tariffs, the transition to electric vehicles and competition from China, Deutsche Bank says. The industry's outlook remains subdued, it adds. "The difficult industry environment has limited impact on Deutsche Bank's automotive and supplier portfolio thus far with only moderate downward rating migrations observed across [a] small portfolio comprising 1.5% of the group's loan book," the bank says. Shares fall 0.3%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1025 GMT - Cathay Pacific's business outlook for 2025 remains balanced with a fair valuation, analysts at Daiwa Capital Markets write in a note. While passenger yields, especially for long-haul routes, will continue normalizing as expected, there is low visibility in the air-cargo market given U.S. tariff uncertainties, they say. However, unit-cost efficiency remains, and the company expects to gain from operating leverage, they add. Daiwa raises its EPS forecast for 2025 by 3% to reflect the stronger-than-expected annual results, but it cuts its projection for 2026 by 4%, taking into account normalizing yields in the passenger and cargo businesses. Daiwa lifts its target price on the stock to HK$10.50 from HK$10.00 while keeping a hold rating. Shares closed at HK$10.30. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

1001 GMT - Capital A's shares have limited upside potential, Citi Research analysts write in a note. The bank downgrades the AirAsia parent's stock to neutral from buy and trims its target price to MYR0.89 from MYR1.11. Chinese tourists' concerns about safety in Thailand will likely weigh on unit Thai AirAsia in 1H, while competitor Cebu Pacific's strength could continue to erode Capital A's market share in the Philippines, Citi says. Ongoing tightness in the aviation industry's supply chain may also pressure Capital A, driving Citi to lower its earnings estimates for 2025 and 2026 by 28% and 10%, respectively. Capital A's non-airline businesses' limited profitability track record and delayed restructuring plans also weigh on the stock, Citi says. Shares last closed at MYR0.805. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0947 GMT - Cathay Pacific's earnings growth will likely remain steady over the next two years despite ticket-pricing pressure and cargo headwinds, DBS Group Research says in a note. Passenger revenue growth, lower ex-fuel unit costs, cheaper fuel and higher contributions from associate Air China could drive core net profit through 2026, analysts Jason Sum and Tabitha Foo say. These factors will likely offset cargo headwinds amid trade tensions and further moderation in passenger yields as supply-demand dynamics normalize, they add. Cathay's 6%-7% dividend yield should help limit share-price downside, they write. DBS raises its target price on the stock to HK$12.20 from HK$12.00 while keeping a buy rating. Shares closed at HK$10.30. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0932 GMT - Rheinmetall's market share is set to grow significantly, supporting long-term revenue projections of between 34 billion and 70 billion euros by 2032, Mwb-research analyst Jens-Peter Rieck says. The German arms maker is at the forefront of catering for European defense needs, securing contracts wins over rival consortium KNDS and high-profile meetings with European leaders. CEO Armin Papperger was the only industrial chief holding direct talks with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky during the Munich Security Conference, Rieck says. Rheinmetall is also demonstrating unmatched production efficiency, and its 2024 earnings call exceeded expectations, he notes. Mwb-research raised its target price for Rheinmetall shares to 1,990 euros from 1,280 euros, and kept its buy rating on the stock. Shares are up 0.5% at 1,271.5 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0914 GMT - Hesai Group's earnings could be stronger this year, given the sensor maker's determination to curb operating expenses, Daiwa analysts write in a note. China's mass-market car market may drive stronger demand for LiDAR products, further boosting the company's performance this year, they say. Daiwa forecasts shipments of 3 million units of advanced driver-assistance systems LiDAR sensors this year in China, mainly driven by mid- to high-end cars with price tags over 150,000 yuan. Meanwhile, the robotics sector could drive some upside surprise for Hesai, as LiDAR is necessary to help robots navigate and avoid obstacles when operating in a complicate space, the investment bank says. Daiwa maintains a buy rating for the stock, which last closed at $22.55. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0913 GMT - Rheinmetall surprised positively on Wednesday with its update on its joint venture with Italian aerospace and defense group Leonardo for the production of combat vehicles, BofA Securities analyst Benjamin Heelan says. Analysts weren't expecting a peak in deliveries by 2031, nor Rheinmetall's announcement of its plan to produce 272 main battle tanks under the joint venture, he says. Heelan expects this peak to boost Rheinmetall's Vehicle Solutions business in 2029-30, and foresees scope for about 14 billion euros in sales by the end of the decade. Rheinmetall is flat at 1,265 euros, while Leonardo is up 0.4% at 43.91 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0825 GMT - Europe's efforts to reduce its reliance on U.S. defense manufacturers will benefit continental European companies over the U.K.'s BAE Systems, Jefferies analysts say. This shift should boost Germany's Rheinmetall and Italy's Leonardo when it comes to land defense, and France's Dassault Aviation--the makers of Rafale fighter jefts--over BAE, the analysts say. Jefferies notes the increased pressure across the continent to spend more on defense. Rheinmetall, its top pick, Renk Group, Dassault Aviation and Leonardo are initiated at a buy rating, while Hensoldt is rated underperform. Rheinmetall and Renk don't appear fully valued, the analysts note. Shares in BAE Systems are up 0.8%, Renk is up 5%, Rheinmetall rises 4.4%, Leonardo is up 0.8%, Dassault Aviation climbs 0.8%, while Hensoldt falls 1.2%. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0427 GMT - Cathay Pacific's earnings beat doesn't compel Citi Research to turn positive on the carrier. The reluctance comes despite Citi's view that mid- to long-haul pricing will unlikely come under significant pressure in 2025 due to tight supply of wide-body jets. Analysts led by Amy Han cite the industry's falling cargo yields on Pacific and Asia-Europe routes this year as a reason for caution. Cathay could face additional downward pressure on demand and yields if the U.S. eventually cancels the de minimis trade exemption for Chinese goods, they say, noting the airline's relatively large exposure to cross-border e-commerce, especially from China. Citi maintains its neutral rating and HK$10.50 target price on the stock. Shares are 5.7% lower at HK$10.28 midday. (farah.elias@wsj.com)

2211 GMT - Military shipbuilder Austal's A$200 million capital raising to expand its shipyard in Mobile, Alabama, doesn't scotch hopes of EPS growth, contends Bell Potter. Analyst Daniel Laing says the dilutive affect of the equity raise and changes to Austal's capex outlook drive EPS downgrades of 7% and 15% in FY 2025 and FY 2026, respectively. Still, Bell Potter says it forecasts "substantial EPS growth in FY 2025/FY 2026/FY 2027 and we believe the reiterated FY 2025 guidance of Ebit not less than A$80 million still looks conservative." It retains a buy call on Austal's stock. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1838 GMT - U.S. auto dealer views on electric vehicle sales improved in 1Q from the final quarter of 2024, but are still weak, Cox Automotive says in its latest Automotive Dealer Sentiment Index. The 1Q index indicates many dealers feel EV sales are worse now than a year ago, Cox says. Expectations for the EV market rose in 1Q, but the score of 40 shows most dealers expect the EV market will decline, rather than grow, over the next three months, according to Cox. The survey also shows dealers continue to view the EV tax credit favorably, citing its benefits for both dealerships and sales, Cox says. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

data.title: BP plans to explore for new oil in Azeri Caspian Sea
data.description:
BP plans to explore for new oil fields in the Azeri Caspian Sea, BP's upstream chief Gordon Birrell told Reuters.

BP is already working on stemming declining output at producing fields in the Caspian Sea, namely the Azeri–Chirag–Gunashli (ACG) complex of oilfields and the Shah Deniz gas field, but had not previously talked about new exploration.

"We are progressing a number of exciting opportunities in Azerbaijan, from further development of ACG and Shah Deniz within existing (production sharing agreements), to accessing discovered (non developed) resources to exploration opportunities," Birrell said this week.

"It remains a very active area for our investments in upstream."

Under a strategy revamp announced last month, BP is increasing spending on its oil and gas business while cutting spending on its low-carbon units.

Azerbaijan's oil output has been declining for several years after the ACG complex passed its peak of 50 million metric tons, or 1 million barrels per day, in 2010. The country also wants to increase its gas exports.

data.title: UPS, FedEx Gain 'Buy' Ratings. There's Upside for Margins. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Emily Dattilo

It's time to bet on a pair of parcel-shipping stocks, Truist Securities says.

Analyst Lucas Servera began coverage of UPS and FedEx with Buy ratings and respective stock-price targets of $140 and $305.

UPS stock gained 1% to $116.39 in Thursday trading, while FedEx edged 0.4% lower to $242.54.

Truist believes UPS can benefit from building out higher-margin areas — small-and-medium businesses and healthcare, saying there is also the potential to improve margins with efforts to boost efficiency and contain costs.

In January, the company said that its business shipping parcels for Amazon.com would be cut in half over the coming 18 months. Though that "is likely to pressure topline results, we believe this is already priced into the stock," the analysts wrote.

FedEx, meanwhile, is undergoing a structural transformation to improve efficiency and margins. In the past, the company has struggled with operating margins compared with UPS because of its separate Express and Ground networks.

A slate of new initiatives should "eliminate redundancies, enhance its cost discipline, and ultimately achieve a leaner, more profitable operation," they wrote.

Write to Emily Dattilo at emily.dattilo@dowjones.com

data.title: Toronto Stocks Decline; Transat Shares Fall on Widened 1Q Loss
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Toronto stocks fell Thursday at midday, weighed down by a broad-based decline across most Canadian sectors. Tech was the biggest decliner in the session, followed by process industries and tech services. Materials stocks were higher, offsetting a greater decline, and commercial services notched minor gains.

On the housing front, building intentions softened by 3.2% in January, with the value of building permits declining at a time of heightened uncertainty for the country in the midst of an escalating trade dispute with the U.S.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index declined by 0.5% to 24305.8 and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 fell by 0.6% to 1460.13.

Shares of integrated tour operator Transat A.T. fell 5.4% to 1.58 Canadian dollars ($1.10) after the company reported a wider first-quarter loss, despite higher revenue.

Other market movers:

Empire's fiscal third-quarter profit rose, benefiting from higher sales and efforts to reduce shrink across its operations. The Canadian grocer said about 12% of its annual sales are related to goods sourced from the U.S., but that it is working to mitigate the worst effects of the escalating tariff situation. Shares fell 2% to C$43.58.

Ballard Power Systems shares rose 2.8% to C$1.81 after the company said its backlog rose by 41%, despite reporting revenue that was nearly halved year over year in the fourth quarter.

Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce's president and chief executive officer, Victor Dodig, is set to retire after 11 years in the lead role. Shares fell 1.1% to C$79.56.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Somnia Expands it's Ecosystem with 14 dApps Across DeFi, Gaming, AI, and Metaverse
data.description:
New York, NY, March 13, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Somnia, the high-performance blockchain designed for fully on-chain applications, is unveiling its initial ecosystem of 14 decentralized applications (dApps) spanning DeFi, gaming, AI, social, metaverse and NFTs.

The first wave of these dApps will go live on the Somnia Testnet, beginning with Playground, a web-based metaverse experience. Over the coming weeks, additional projects will be activated, with deep dives into each initiative to showcase their capabilities and impact on the Somnia ecosystem.

A Diverse Ecosystem of High-Impact dApps

The Somnia ecosystem is built to support real-time, mass-scale decentralized applications. The launch includes:

DeFi & Cross-Chain Liquidity

  • Salt – A self-custodial treasury coordination platform powered by decentralized multi-party computation (MPC). Salt is different from many other MPC wallets because key material is managed and secured using the blockchain itself making Salt wallets truly self-custodial.
  • Standard – A fully on-chain central limit order book (CLOB) and perpetual exchange.

AI & Social Identity

  • Sogni AI – A decentralized AI network leveraging distributed computing for high-speed image generation. Users contribute device power in "Worker Mode" to earn Sogni tokens.
  • ForU AI – The Real-World AI Engine for On-Chain Communities, powering AI-DiDs that bring digital identity to life. Create, own, and tokenize AI Agents that evolve, compete, and drive real-world impact for both Web2 and Web3 communities.
  • Otomata – A no-code platform for building agent-based dApps with revenue-sharing mechanics.
  • LootMogul - LootMogul is an AI-powered fan-centric gaming and e-commerce platform that enables fans to create, play, and monetize their own games and merch using their favorite athletes and sports teams.

Gaming & On-Chain Virtual Worlds

  • YOM – A decentralized gaming and cloud streaming platform leveraging P2P Edge (DePIN) technology, enabling AAA-quality game streaming to any device.
  • Lucky Adventurer’s Gambit (Hexaplay) – A turn-based RPG featuring strategic on-chain trading mechanics and PvP combat.
  • Maelstrom – A tactical naval warfare battle royale game with fantasy elements and deep customization.

Metaverse & Interoperability

  • MSquared – A platform for building high-density virtual worlds with built-in interoperability, enabling users to transfer digital assets across multiple metaverses. MSquared is also enhancing developer tooling for immersive on-chain experiences, simplifying real-time blockchain interactions.
  • Playground – A web-based metaverse platform for building and exploring interoperable experiences.
  • Dream Builder – A 3D asset creation tool allowing users to design NFT-based avatars, objects, and worlds.
  • MML – Open-source technology for building multiplayer metaverse experiences using web standards.

NFTs & Digital Ownership

  • Quills – An on-chain digital collectibles project. 

Building Momentum for a Fully On-Chain Future

“This is just the beginning,” said Somnia founder Paul Thomas. “With over 14 dApps already in motion across DeFi, gaming, metaverse, AI, and NFTs, Somnia is demonstrating that fully on-chain applications are not just possible—they are happening now. We are building the foundation for a scalable, decentralized ecosystem that enables high-performance applications at a level never seen before in Web3.”

As the Somnia Testnet evolves, more projects will be integrated, further expanding its ecosystem. Developers and creators interested in building high-performance, real-time applications on a fully on-chain platform are invited to explore the Testnet and contribute to the growing Somnia community.

For more information and updates, visit Somnia.Network

About Somnia

Somnia is the fastest and most cost-effective EVM Layer-1 blockchain, capable of processing over 1 million transactions per second (tps) with sub-second finality. Somnia’s new multi-stream consensus technology achieves sub-second block certainty and higher transaction throughput. Sequential execution and compression algorithms effectively handle high-density scenarios, increasing the amount of data transferred between nodes by 10-20x. The custom database IceDB achieves 15-100 nanosecond read/write times, reducing transaction costs to less than a penny. This makes Somnia an ideal platform for building large-scale, real-time applications in games, social, metaverse, finance, and other fields, serving millions of users, all on-chain, making EVM more efficient than ever before.

It is strongly recommended you practice due diligence, including consultation with a professional financial advisor, before investing in or trading cryptocurrency and securities.

John Vibes

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1204 ET - Orders for new dry-bulk ships dropped 92% year-over-year in the first two months of 2025 to the lowest in at least 30 years, shipowners' association Bimco says. While contracting was low in January, no new bulkers at all were ordered in February. "Weak freight rates, high newbuilding prices, long lead times and uncertainty are likely discouraging contracting," says Filipe Gouveia, Bimco's shipping analysis manager. Strong contracting for containerships and tankers, which use the same shipyards, has kept newbuild prices high and extended lead times, Bimco adds. (mark.long@wsj.com)

1105 ET - U.S. natural gas futures turn higher after a weekly storage draw lands above expectations, widening the deficit over the five-year average and the year-earlier level. Natural gas inventories declined by 62 billion cubic feet last week to 1,698 Bcf, the EIA reports, which was 230 Bcf less than the 2020-2024 average for the week and down by 628 Bcf from a year before. Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey had forecast an average draw of 54 Bcf. The Nymex front month is up 1.9% at $4.162/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1045 ET - Lean hog futures on the CME are down 1.5% in early trading, this after the USDA reported lower export sales in its weekly report. The USDA says that export sales for the week ended March 6 fell 52% for the previous week, to 20,300 metric tons for delivery in 2025. That's a marketing year low, the USDA says. Mexico was the leading buyer for the week, totaling 6,300 tons. Other leading buyers include Japan, South Korea, and Colombia. Live cattle futures are also down in morning trade, falling 0.2%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0945 ET - Oil futures are giving back some of the previous session's gains with market concerns over the demand outlook and the potential economic impact of trade tariffs weighing on sentiment. The IEA lowered its demand growth estimate for this year to 1.03 million barrels a day from 1.1 million b/d, citing trade tensions. "The tariff trade war saga continues to weigh on prices and a peace deal with Russia is very probable, and with peace will likely come added Russian crude barrels to the world market," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "Add in the latest selloff in the stock market and near-term demand remains vulnerable." WTI is down 1% at $67.03 a barrel and Brent is off 0.9% at $70.34 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0931 ET - President Trump's focus on annexing Canada to make it the 51st U.S. state has put the northern nation's wealth of natural resources in the spotlight, but just what is that worth? Based on data from Statistics Canada, the country's mineral and energy reserves and timber combined accounted for nearly C$1.3 trillion in the last quarter, or 7.5% of the country's national wealth. Canadian exports of minerals, energy and timber represent a big contributor to its economy and to the economic activity of countries who benefit from this trade, including the U.S., the data agency notes. It says 2024 data on Canadian international merchandise trade shows selected domestic exports of minerals, energy and timber products accounted for over C$300 billion, with nearly three-quarters of this destined for the U.S. on a customs basis. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0925 ET - Brazilian agricultural agency Conab has raised its projections for the size of this year's crops. Conab says that the Brazilian soybean crop will hit 167.4 million metric tons. That's up 13% from last year, and up from 166 million tons projected last month. The agency attributed the uptick in soybean production to higher-than-expected yields for crops, thanks to a timely decrease in precipitation allowing the late-stage crop to flourish in its final stages in some Brazilian states. Corn is projected at 122.8 million tons, which is up 800,000 tons from Conab's previous forecast. "After a slower start to the harvest due to planting delays and excessive rainfall in January, the reduction in rainfall in February led to a significant increase in the harvested area," says the agency. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0913 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are lower for a third consecutive session as the market has stopped ignoring bearish fundamentals such as buoyant production and warm March weather that's keeping a lid on demand. Focus is on the EIA's storage report, which is expected to show the smallest weekly inventory withdrawal since the start of the year. The report "may be a bellwether after back-to-back bearish surprises," says Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics, noting that prospects of a net injection in the remainder of March could shrink the deficit against the five-year average by 150 Bcf. The Nymex front month is down 1.9% at $4.007/mmmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0750 ET - Gold futures rise on weaker U.S. inflation data and increased plans from central banks to buy bullion. Futures are up 0.35% at $2,956.90 a troy ounce, near all-time highs of $2,974/oz. The precious metal gained after Wednesday's U.S. Consumer Price Index data release showed weaker-than-expected inflation, SP Angel analysts say in a note. This raises hopes the Fed will ease monetary policy, a boon for non-interest bearing bullion. Gold is also gaining on safe-haven demand amid market volatility, SP Angel says. Meanwhile, Bolivia's state gold trader outlined plans to quadruple gold purchases this year to boost the central bank's foreign reserves--equivalent to $1 billion of gold--while the Chinese central bank has purchased gold for four consecutive months, analysts add. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0706 ET - Base metal prices aren't moving much but copper and aluminum remain up roughly 3% on month. LME three-month copper is down 0.3% at $9,757 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum is down 0.8% at $2,677.50 a ton. Price moves in aluminum and steel have been broadly muted since U.S. President Trump imposed 25% tariffs on imports of the metal products Wednesday, unsurprising given the move was well-telegraphed, MUFG analysts say in a note. However, the longer-term consequences of the levies might breed further volatility in metals markets, analysts say. A shortage of domestic aluminum production is prompting higher premiums for physical deliveries in the U.S., with prices on the U.S. commodities exchange Comex significantly above LME prices. Meanwhile, U.S. steel producers could gain from the higher steel tariffs--though low capacity utilization could cap gains, MUFG adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0626 ET - Most commodity prices are expected to decline in the second half of the year as a result of global trade tensions and a slowdown in economic growth, according to Macquarie Research analysts. "U.S. growth appears to have slowed sharply in early 2025 with tariffs and policy uncertainty weighing on the outlook," they say. "Our base case is for weaker global goods demand and industrial production growth to hurt first use commodities consumption." Macquarie forecasts Brent crude and WTI at an average of $66 a barrel and $62 a barrel in the second quarter. In the second half of the year, the benchmarks are projected at $68 and $64 a barrel, respectively. The most notable exception is gold, with prices expected to remain high and average $2,951 a troy ounce in 2025, the analysts say.(giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0622 ET - Palm oil ended higher, with the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for May delivery rising 52 ringgit to 4,539 ringgit/ton. The commodity faced bargain hunting after recent declines, said Sathia Varqa, senior analyst at Fastmarkets Palm Oil Analytics. Meanwhile, CPO futures should be mildly supported by Malaysia's shrinking palm oil inventory and higher palm oil demand during the Ramadan fasting month, Apex Securities analyst Steven Chong wrote in a recent note.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0547 ET - A slowdown in U.S. inflation has temporarily eased pressure on oil prices. But uncertainties around trade and geopolitical developments are limiting further gains, according to Pepperstone's Quasar Elizundia. "The recent slowdown in U.S. inflation, evidenced by the lowest CPI growth rate in four months, has fueled market expectations of a more accommodative Fed in future monetary decisions," the research strategist says. However, talks for a ceasefire in Ukraine and the rollout of U.S. trade tariffs continue to weigh on sentiment. Meanwhile, EIA data showed mixed results, adding to uncertainties over demand. "The combination of economic, geopolitical, and trade factors continues to create a highly uncertain environment," Elizundia says. "Markets should brace for more turbulence in the coming months." Brent crude is down 0.4% at $70.66 a barrel, while WTI falls 0.5% to $67.31 a barrel. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Treasury Secretary Bessent says he's not concerned about the current stock market volatility
data.description:
By Greg Robb

Treasury secretary says he's 'not concerned about a little bit of volatility'

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Thursday downplayed the recent weakness in the stock market, saying the White House was focused on the long term.

"I'm not concerned about a little bit of volatility over three weeks," Bessent said in an interview on CNBC.

The S&P 500 SPX is down 6% since the beginning of the month.

Bessent said stocks remained "a safe and great investment over the long term. "

The Treasury secretary also said a possible government shutdown would be "incredibly disruptive."

Asked if a shutdown would cause economic harm, Bessent replied: "It can't be good."

-Greg Robb

NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / March 13, 2025 / St. Clair Orthopaedics & Sports Medicine recently disclosed that it suffered a data breach that compromised the sensitive personal and protected health data of individuals. This data breach has led to concerns over the security of sensitive personal and protected health information entrusted to SCOSM.

WHAT'S THIS ABOUT?

On November 24, 2024, SCOSM became aware of a security incident within its IT network. SCOSM immediately launched an investigation and engaged cybersecurity experts to assess the nature and scope of the unauthorized network access. On December 9, 2024, the investigation determined that some impacted locations contained patient information. After completing the investigation on December 20, 2024, SCOSM conducted a comprehensive review of the compromised files to confirm the specific information affected.

The review concluded on January 29, 2025, at which time SCOSM determined that patient information was involved and began notifying affected individuals. On January 30, 2025, SCOSM filed a notice with the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Office for Civil Rights (OCR). data.title: 'Living Your Legacy, Every Day'
data.description:
Retired Lt. General A.C. Roper was honored at Regions Bank's History in Motion celebration for his life of leadership and service.

By Candace Higginbotham

NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / March 13, 2025 / "He's not like us," said Leroy Abrahams, Regions head of Community Engagement, as he began his opening remarks at the Regions Bank History in Motion celebration.

Above, left: Regions Head of Community Engagement and retired Lt. Gen. A.C. Roper. Below, right: Birmingham Mayor Randall Woodfin.

"For most of us, success in life requires intense focus on one discipline and, if we're fortunate, excelling in that capacity. But retired Lt. Gen. A.C. Roper is the Bo Jackson of service. He knows law enforcement; he knows national security and he knows ministry.

"He does more before 9 a.m. than most of us will do all day!"

For the past 16 years, Regions Bank has recognized Black History Month with its History in Motion event, which honors distinguished leaders with ties to the company's home state of Alabama. The award recognizes contributions these individuals have made to our communities and our nation.

During the recent celebration Abrahams, along with more than 200 family members, friends, elected officials, community and religious leaders and corporate executives, gathered at Regions Center in Birmingham to honor the life and career of retired Lt Gen. A.C. Roper, the first African American in the Army Reserve's 116-year history to achieve the three-star rank.

As Abrahams mentioned, national security isn't Roper's only specialty. He also spent 33 years in law enforcement, including serving as Chief of Police in Birmingham for 10 years. Abrahams noted that Roper's commitment went beyond service - it was "knowing" his field. Roper earned a master's degree in criminal justice from the University of Alabama, graduated from the FBI National Academy and the FBI National Executive institute, served as adjunct professor of Criminal Justice and has been an executive board member of the FBI joint terrorism task force.

Roper's history-making 42-year military career includes even more impressive academic and service credentials, including earning a master's in strategic studies from the U.S. Army War College and serving in prestigious positions such as Vice Commander of the American Element of the North America Aerospace Defense Command, Deputy Commander of the U.S. Northern Command, and Deputy Commanding General of the U.S. Army Reserve.

His grandfather would be so proud.

Leroy Abrahams, Regions head of Community Engagement

Roper deployed to Saudi Arabia during Operation Desert Shield and to Afghanistan in support of Operation Enduring Freedom. His numerous awards and decorations include the Defense Distinguished Service Medal, Distinguished Service Medal, Legion of Merit with two Oak Leaf Clusters, Bronze Star Medal, Meritorious Service Medal with three Oak Leaf Clusters, Joint Service Commendation Medal, Combat Action Badge, among others.

He retired in 2024 as the Deputy Commander of U.S. Northern Command, overseeing homeland defense and disaster response. In a video tribute, Roper reflected on several meaningful milestones of his life, including achieving the three-star rank. "I can't think of anything better than defending this homeland that's so precious to us," he said.

"His grandfather would be so proud," Abrahams said, explaining that Corporal William Roper served in the 366th Infantry Regiment, 92nd Infantry Division in World War I. He deployed to France in a segregated Army unit known as Buffalo Soldiers and fought in the Meuse-Argonne Campaign of 1918.

"Cpl. Roper and the other 380,000 Black service members fought valiantly to restore freedoms for the French that these service members didn't enjoy in America at that time," Abrahams said.

Roper shared that his grandfather, as well as the grandmother who raised him, instilled from an early age a commitment to excellence and the discipline to achieve great things. The outcome of those early life lessons became clearer as History in Motion guests learned more about his personal life and his motivation for leadership.

Birmingham Mayor Randall Woodfin described Roper as "a servant leader, a man of passion and purpose" and credited him for "creating a fairer, safer and more united Birmingham" during his time as police chief.

Those attributes prompted the mayor to tap Roper last year as an advisor for a commission tasked with identifying ways to reduce violent crime in the city. Roper and the committee recently produced a comprehensive report outlining key recommendations, which according to Woodfin, the city immediately began implementing.

I've accomplished much, but there's so much more to do.

A.C. Roper, Retired Lt. General

Roper accepted the History in Motion award with the humility, gratitude and grace one would expect. "We don't serve for recognition," he began. "I've accomplished much, but there's so much more to do."

Roper recalled his high school memory book, where he recorded what he wanted to accomplish in his life. He wrote that he wanted to be a police officer, wanted to join the military and wanted to marry "a sweet, pretty women who understands me."

He didn't realize then that he was writing his own roadmap. "You can set the course for your life," he said. "Then, it's just a matter of making the right decisions to get there."

A critical juncture in his journey was meeting Edith, his wife of 40 years. According to Roper, his police and military careers were a team effort, and she has been instrumental in all the career decisions and achievements.

"My family is my ‘why'" he said. "I often lean on a Gale Sayers quote: God is first, family is second and I am third. That's my mantra."

Last May, Alabama Rep. Terri Sewell spoke on the House Floor to honor Roper for his miliary service and law enforcement career.

"Throughout his exemplary career, Lieutenant General Roper moved quickly up the ranks and broke down barriers for African Americans serving in the armed forces," Sewell said. "I got to know Lieutenant General Roper during his time as the Chief of Police for the Birmingham Police Department. I was immediately impressed by his firm yet compassionate leadership style that commanded the respect of his fellow officers and endeared him to the Birmingham community.

"Lieutenant General Roper's reputation has always been one of great purpose and great passion. He is an honorable man, guided by an abiding faith in God and a love of country."

Roper's commitment to servant leadership continues today as he and Edith launched Rest in Grace Ministries. Roper also works as a consultant and will be publishing a book on leadership in the coming months.

Though he's proud of his accomplishments and the people he's inspired during two remarkable careers, Roper is most looking forward to what he calls "Chapter 3" of his life. And this chapter - filled with coaching, mentoring and empowering others to achieve balanced living, impactful leadership and a lasting legacy - may indeed be his best yet.

Legacy is something he's often asked about. "People think legacy is something that's left behind after a person passes away," Roper said. "But I say you're living your legacy every day."

data.title: Wine Tariffs Could Make Focused Impact on EU Countries — Market Talk
data.description: President Trump's threat to levy a 200% tariff on wine and spirits coming from the EU would have a big impact on two EU countries in particular. According to data from the U.S. International Trade Commission, the U.S. imported the most wine from France and Italy in 2024 — totaling roughly $4.7 billion, or 70% of all of the wine imported into the U.S. that year. Wine accounted for roughly half of France's shipments of alcohol to the U.S. in 2024, making it the second-largest alcohol exporter for the year — with Mexico the leading exporter at $12.9 billion, according to the USITC. data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1209 ET - President Trump's threat to levy a 200% tariff on wine and spirits coming from the EU would have a big impact on two EU countries in particular. According to data from the U.S. International Trade Commission, the U.S. imported the most wine from France and Italy in 2024 — totaling roughly $4.7 billion, or 70% of all of the wine imported into the U.S. that year. Wine accounted for roughly half of France's shipments of alcohol to the U.S. in 2024, making it the second-largest alcohol exporter for the year — with Mexico the leading exporter at $12.9 billion, according to the USITC. Trump's tariff threat came after the EU included U.S. whiskey in its round of retaliatory tariffs. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1032 ET - The idea of another Plaza Accord-style agreement to depreciate the dollar seems outdated, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in a note. The 1985 Plaza Accord placed a heavy emphasis on direct foreign-exchange interventions. Persistent multilateral FX intervention "seems archaic today with Japan the only large G-10 economy to have dallied with it in recent years," the analysts say. "Also, while many see the dollar as expensive, it's far from clear that policymakers all agree this is the case to an extent where global corrective policy is needed." The U.S. current account deficit is more muted compared to levels in mid-2000s. This points to bilateral deals in response to U.S. pressure as opposed to any multilateral agreement, they say. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1023 ET - As trade tensions increase between the U.S. and China, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent says that he has had one conversation with a Chinese counterpart in recent weeks. In a CNBC interview, Bessent says that they discussed Chinese purchases of sanctioned oil and the smuggling of fentanyl into the U.S. Trump will likely be engaged in high-level trade talks with China at some point, Bessent says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1019 ET - Trouble in the auto industry poses a growing risk to Deutsche Bank's lending portfolio to the sector, but the impact so far is limited, the German bank says in its annual report. The lender says it identified the auto industry, as well as commercial real estate and climate risk, as focus areas in its 2024 credit-risk management assessment. The European auto industry faces challenges including potential U.S. tariffs, the transition to electric vehicles and competition from China, Deutsche Bank says. The industry's outlook remains subdued, it adds. "The difficult industry environment has limited impact on Deutsche Bank's automotive and supplier portfolio thus far with only moderate downward rating migrations observed across [a] small portfolio comprising 1.5% of the group's loan book," the bank says. Shares fall 0.3%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1017 ET - Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent says in an interview on CNBC that U.S. trading partners, not the U.S. itself, will face the worst consequences of the burgeoning trade war. "It's the surplus country that takes the greatest hit," Bessent says. "In the event that there's a back-and-forth with tariffs, I would council these government leaders that they're on the losing side of this argument." Bessent has said that the transition to President Trump's tariff plans and fiscal agenda will bring an economic "detox," but says in the interview that doesn't have to be a euphemism for recession. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1016 ET - Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent says that as the stock market falls on whipsawing tariff news, the Trump administration is more concerned with a long-term view. "If you start looking at micro-horizons, stocks become very risky," Bessent says in an interview on CNBC. "We're focused over the medium to long term." Bessent also encourages Democrats to work with Republicans on avoiding a government shutdown, saying that a shutdown could be "incredibly disruptive." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

0931 ET - President Trump's focus on annexing Canada to make it the 51st U.S. state has put the northern nation's wealth of natural resources in the spotlight, but just what is that worth? Based on data from Statistics Canada, the country's mineral and energy reserves and timber combined accounted for nearly C$1.3 trillion in the last quarter, or 7.5% of the country's national wealth. Canadian exports of minerals, energy and timber represent a big contributor to its economy and to the economic activity of countries who benefit from this trade, including the U.S., the data agency notes. It says 2024 data on Canadian international merchandise trade shows selected domestic exports of minerals, energy and timber products accounted for over C$300 billion, with nearly three-quarters of this destined for the U.S. on a customs basis. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0911 ET - Empire says Canadians are rallying behind the Buy Canadian movement as the U.S. trade war with Canada continues. In an earnings call, executives say that sales of Canadian products are out-pacing those from the U.S. The Canadian grocer has been nurturing the sentiment with specific signage and marketing, which is gaining more traction. Half-way through the current fourth fiscal quarter, the executives say that there is similar momentum to 3Q, "things are normal, no cautious behavior," they say. "We're operating in strange and unpleasant time, but Canadians are coming together," they note. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0854 ET - Canadian grocery giant Empire says that it is quickly finding alternatives for its U.S.-sourced products. In an earnings call, executives say that about 12% of its products originate in the U.S., but this is "rapidly dropping." Demand has been strong for Canadian and non-U.S. products, they say, and the company has been adjusting to this. They say that there is an alternative to U.S. products in nearly all categories, but that fresh produce remains the most difficult to replace. Here, Empire thinks that prices will be passed onto consumers, but executives say that their positive relationships with suppliers, even U.S. ones, may help find a compromise. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0845 ET - Treasury yields and the dollar lose some steam as U.S. producer prices cool and jobless claims tick lower. February PPI was flat, while economists surveyed by WSJ expected a 0.3% uptick. January's pace is revised to 0.6% from 0.4%. Some analysts thought tariffs would bring an upward surprise. Weekly jobless claims were 220,000, down from an upwardly revised 222,000 and consensus estimate of 225,000. The data could ease recession and inflation fears, ahead of next week's Fed meeting. President Trump threatens the E.U. with tariffs as high as 200% on alcohol. The 10-year yield is at 4.344% and the two-year at 4.009%, while the WSJ Dollar Index rises 0.1%, all off early highs. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0841 ET - Shares in French distillers fall after President Trump said he could impose tariffs on French alcoholic products. The EU said Wednesday it planned 50% tariffs on imports of American whiskey starting April 1. "If this Tariff is not removed immediately, the U.S. will shortly place a 200% Tariff on all WINES, CHAMPAGNES, & ALCOHOLIC PRODUCTS COMING OUT OF FRANCE AND OTHER E.U. REPRESENTED COUNTRIES," Trump said on Truth Social. Shares in Moet & Chandon owner LVMH fell 1.9%, while those in Pernod Ricard and Remy Cointreau dropped by 3.3% and 4.1%, respectively. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0706 ET - Base metal prices aren't moving much but copper and aluminum remain up roughly 3% on month. LME three-month copper is down 0.3% at $9,757 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum is down 0.8% at $2,677.50 a ton. Price moves in aluminum and steel have been broadly muted since U.S. President Trump imposed 25% tariffs on imports of the metal products Wednesday, unsurprising given the move was well-telegraphed, MUFG analysts say in a note. However, the longer-term consequences of the levies might breed further volatility in metals markets, analysts say. A shortage of domestic aluminum production is prompting higher premiums for physical deliveries in the U.S., with prices on the U.S. commodities exchange Comex significantly above LME prices. Meanwhile, U.S. steel producers could gain from the higher steel tariffs--though low capacity utilization could cap gains, MUFG adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)
